question: Q2: Referrals & Viral Loops

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you’ve implemented a referral program.
sections.hint: Giving users a reward when they invite a friend — credits, discounts, or free features
no_hands: What stopped you from trying referrals so far?
Do they feel too complex, risky, or unnecessary?
If you added one tomorrow, what kind of reward would you start with?
hands_up: What pushed you to introduce referrals?
Which reward type worked best for you?
What user behavior surprised you most?
What was the toughest issue — fraud, tracking, or low interest?
If you had to design it again, what would you approach differently?
follow_ups: Who tried a reward that users ignored or disliked?
Did referrals create only a short-term spike, or did it last?
Who has used a very different type of incentive?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you’ve built a viral loop into your app.
sections.hint: Features that naturally encourage sharing — Duolingo streaks, game invites, or working together
no_hands: Why do viral features feel like a bad fit for your app?
Where could sharing feel natural, not forced?
What’s the simplest viral element you could imagine testing?
hands_up: What viral feature did you build, and what happened?
Did it bring in new users, or mostly engage existing ones?
How did you make sharing feel useful instead of spammy?
What technical setup helped or caused problems?
follow_ups: Who added a viral element that failed — why?
Who found another growth method that outperformed viral loops?
Who has an example where viral sharing had a surprising impact?

wrap_up: What’s one thing you’d include in a referral program today?
Do you think referrals/loops work for all apps, or only some?
What other growth tactic worked better for you?
